optimal_conditions.watering_frequency: Deep watering 1-2 times per week (keep soil consistently moist but not waterlogged)
optimal_conditions.soil_type: Well-drained, fertile, loamy soil rich in organic matter (pH 6.0 - 6.8)
optimal_conditions.sunlight: Full sun (6 - 8 hours of direct sunlight daily)

symptoms: Circular dark spots with concentric rings ('target board' pattern) appearing first on older lower leaves.
Yellowing of surrounding leaf tissue, leading to premature leaf drop.
Dark, sunken, leathery lesions near the stem end of the fruit.
Dark, dry spots on stems with similar concentric rings.
organic_control: Apply copper-based organic fungicides early in the season or at first sign of symptoms.
Spray leaves with a diluted solution of Bacillus subtilis or neem oil.
Apply organic mulch around the base of the plant to prevent soil-borne spores from splashing onto leaves.
Prune lower branches to improve airflow and keep foliage dry.
chemical_control: Apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or difenoconazole-based commercial fungicides.
Rotate chemical groups to prevent resistance development.
prevention_tips: Practice a 3-year crop rotation schedule excluding solanaceous crops (peppers, eggplants, potatoes).
Water at the base of the plant using drip irrigation rather than overhead sprinklers.
Always source certified disease-free seeds and seedlings.
Sanitize garden tools, tomato cages, and stakes with a 10% bleach solution.

diseases.causal_agent: Virus (Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus - TYLCV, transmitted by Whiteflies)
symptoms: Severe stunting of the entire plant, especially if infected at an early stage.
Leaves turn pale green or yellow along the margins and interveinal areas.
Upward curling, cupping, and crinkling of leaf margins.
Leaves become abnormally small, leathery, and thick.
Failure to set fruit or heavy blossom drop, resulting in minimal yield.
organic_control: Install yellow sticky traps to capture and monitor whitefly vectors.
Apply insecticidal soaps, horticultural oils, or neem oil to suppress whitefly populations.
Release natural predators of whiteflies, such as lacewings or predatory mites (Phytoseiidae).
Remove and immediately destroy infected plants to prevent further spread.
chemical_control: Apply systemic insecticides, such as imidacloprid or acetamiprid, to control whitefly populations.
Use synthetic pyrethroids or organophosphates for quick knockdown of adult whitefly populations.
prevention_tips: Grow whitefly-resistant tomato cultivars (e.g., 'Tygress', 'Tribute').
Use fine mesh insect netting, floating row covers, or silver reflective mulches to deter whiteflies.
Keep the surrounding field free of weeds and solanaceous hosts that harbor both the virus and vector.
Observe a strict host-free window between planting seasons.

optimal_conditions.watering_frequency: 1-2 inches of water per week (maintain consistent moisture, especially during tuber bulking)
optimal_conditions.soil_type: Loose, well-drained, sandy loam soil with high organic content (pH 4.8 - 5.5 to reduce scab disease)
optimal_conditions.sunlight: Full sun (6 - 8 hours of direct sunlight daily)

symptoms: Water-soaked, dark green to black lesions starting on leaf tips and margins.
A white, fuzzy mold growth on the undersides of leaves during humid or wet conditions.
Rapid collapse, rotting, and blackening of the entire plant canopy under high humidity.
Copper-brown, dry, granular rot or skin discoloration on tubers.
organic_control: Apply copper-based organic formulations or copper hydroxide sprays proactively.
Utilize bio-fungicides containing Bacillus subtilis or Streptomyces lydicus.
Prune infected foliage immediately and bury or burn it (do not compost infested material).
chemical_control: Use systemic and contact fungicides containing metalaxyl, fluopicolide, chlorothalonil, or cymoxanil.
Execute spray programs starting before the canopy closes or when localized weather forecasts warn of blight conditions.
prevention_tips: Only plant certified disease-free seed tubers.
Destroy all cull piles and volunteer potato plants which serve as primary inoculum sources.
Avoid overhead irrigation, particularly in the late afternoon or evening.
Harvest only after the vine canopy has been completely dead for at least 14 days to minimize tuber infection.

symptoms: Circular, raised, corky, dark brown lesions on the surface of the tubers.
Sunken, pitted pits on tuber skin which reduce aesthetic and market quality.
Slightly brownish russeting or star-shaped lesions on young developing tubers.
Leaves, stems, and blossoms typically remain entirely asymptomatic.
organic_control: Amended soil with organic sulfur to keep the soil pH within a strongly acidic range (pH 5.0 - 5.2).
Maintain soil moisture near field capacity during tuber initiation and early development (first 4 - 6 weeks).
Apply composted pine needles or peat moss to naturally lower localized root-zone pH.
chemical_control: Seed tuber treatment with fludioxonil or mancozeb prior to planting.
Apply soil fumigants or chloropicrin in severe commercially monocultured fields.
prevention_tips: Rotate potato fields with non-hosts, such as alfalfa, rye, oats, or corn.
Avoid planting highly susceptible potato varieties; opt for resistant cultivars (e.g., 'Russet Burbank', 'Norgold').
Avoid using alkaline soil amendments, uncomposted animal manures, wood ash, or lime.
Utilize cover crops that do not host Streptomyces bacterial strains.

optimal_conditions.watering_frequency: Flooded water depth of 5-10 cm during vegetative phase (requires heavy water availability)
optimal_conditions.soil_type: Heavy clay or clay-loamy soils with poor drainage to retain surface water (pH 5.5 - 6.5)
optimal_conditions.sunlight: High intensity full sun (8 hours daily for maximum tillering and grain setting)

symptoms: Spindle-shaped, diamond-shaped, or elliptical lesions on leaf blades with gray centers and reddish-brown borders.
Lesions expand rapidly and coalesce, causing entire leaves to dry, wither, and die.
Brownish-black discoloration and necrosis of the panicle base node (known as 'neck blast' or 'rotten neck'), leading to empty grains.
Lesions on the nodes of leaf sheaths, causing the stem to break easily.
organic_control: Apply bio-insecticides/fungicides containing Pseudomonas fluorescens formulation as a seed treatment and foliar spray.
Apply silicon-rich mineral amendments (such as diatomaceous earth or rice husk ash) to strengthen host cell walls.
Maintain continuous shallow flooding of the rice fields to reduce spore germination rates on dry soil.
chemical_control: Apply systemic fungicides containing tricyclazole, azoxystrobin, edifenphos, or carbendazim.
Use prophylactic seed treatments and early vegetative stage prophylactic sprays.
prevention_tips: Plant blast-resistant rice cultivars developed for the local agro-ecological zone.
Avoid over-application of nitrogen fertilizers, which triggers lush vegetative growth susceptible to fungal penetration.
Enforce uniform sowing dates and optimal spacing to avoid over-congested canopies.
Burn or deeply plow post-harvest rice straw and stubble to eliminate overwintering mycelia.

symptoms: Wavy, yellow to straw-colored lesions starting on leaf tips and margins, progressing downward.
Young seedlings show rapid structural wilting, drying, and rolling of leaves (called 'kresek' stage).
Droplets of milky or yellow bacterial ooze (exudate) on leaves under humid morning conditions.
Infected leaves eventually turn fully grayish-white, dry out, and lose photosynthetic capacity.
organic_control: Spray freshly made wild mint (Mentha arvensis) leaf extracts or garlic bulb extracts, which possess natural antibacterial activity.
Apply copper-oxychloride formulations combined with organic sticker agents.
Drain the rice paddies temporarily to dry field moisture and disrupt bacterial reproduction cycles.
chemical_control: Spray with streptocycline (streptomycin sulfate + tetracycline hydrochloride combination).
Apply copper-based chemicals like copper hydroxide or copper sulfate heptahydrate.
prevention_tips: Construct proper field drainage channels to prevent prolonged stagnation of deep waters.
Minimize mechanical damage to plants during transplanting or weeding to prevent bacterial entry wounds.
Optimize nitrogen dosing by dividing it into 3 split applications based on leaf color chart monitoring.
Keep bunds and irrigation canals clean of wild grass hosts (like Leersia oryzoides).

optimal_conditions.watering_frequency: Moderate water (Needs 350-500 mm rainfall/irrigation, timed to crown root initiation, jointing, and flowering stages)
optimal_conditions.soil_type: Well-aerated, deep, rich loamy and clayey soils with high water retention capacity (pH 6.0 - 7.0)
optimal_conditions.sunlight: Full sun (6 - 8 hours of direct, high-intensity sunlight during head filling)

symptoms: Small, circular to oval, orange-brown pustules (uredinia) scattered randomly across leaf blades and sheaths.
Pustules rupture the leaf epidermis, releasing dusty orange spore masses that stain clothing and equipment.
Chlorotic yellowing of leaf areas surrounding rust pustules.
Premature drying, curling of leaves, and reduction in the size and weight of kernels.
organic_control: Foliar spray with fermented cow urine solution or neem seed kernel extract (NSKE).
Apply sulfur-based dust or organic wettable micro-refined sulfur.
Implement high-density crop polycultures to break fungal spore dispersal lines.
chemical_control: Foliar applications of triazole fungicides (such as propiconazole, tebuconazole, or triadimefon).
Apply strobilurins (e.g., azoxystrobin) in early infection stages to protect flag leaves.
prevention_tips: Grow durable, multi-gene resistant wheat seed cultivars (containing active Sr or Lr genes).
Eliminate alternate weed hosts and volunteer self-sown wheat during the fallow period.
Sow seeds early in the planting window to allow plants to mature before warm weather triggers peak rust germination.

symptoms: White, fluffy, cottony or powdery fungal patches (mycelium) on the lower leaves and stems first.
These patches expand, turning powdery grey or light brown as they age.
Small, dark brown to black spherical structures (cleistothecia) develop inside the aging grey patches.
Severely affected leaves lose color, wither, dry out, and suffer from photosynthetic failure.
organic_control: Spray with a solution of potassium bicarbonate or sodium bicarbonate mixed with soft soap as a spreading agent.
Apply diluted milk/whey sprays (1:9 ratio with water), which generate free radicals on leaves under sunlight to kill spores.
Prune over-dense, shade-heavy field margins to increase overall sunlight penetration.
chemical_control: Use systemic fungicides like triadimenol, propiconazole, flutriafol, or fenpropimorph.
Ensure protection spray covers the vital upper flag leaf during the boot stage.
prevention_tips: Avoid high seed sowing densities to facilitate wind circulation in the crop canopy.
Apply balanced nitrogen inputs (excess nitrogen encourages fleshy, soft tissue prone to mildew infection).
Plant resistant crop varieties and practice field rotations with non-cereal crops.

optimal_conditions.watering_frequency: 1.5-2 inches of water per week (extremely critical during silking, tasseling, and cob development)
optimal_conditions.soil_type: Deep, loose, fertile, sandy-loam or silty-loam soil rich in organic matter (pH 5.8 - 6.8)
optimal_conditions.sunlight: Full sun (Requires 8 - 10 hours of intense sunlight daily, very sensitive to shade)

symptoms: Small, tan or brown spots that expand into long, narrow, rectangular lesions restricted by leaf veins.
Lesions display sharp, parallel borders and turn gray-brown or ash-gray under humid conditions.
Severe lesions merge, causing extensive blighting, death of whole leaves, and structural lodging of the stalks.
Early defoliation leads to under-filled cobs and light, chaffy grain.
organic_control: Apply organic compost tea sprays populated with beneficial organisms to crowd out fungal pathogens on leaves.
Incorporate beneficial Trichoderma fungi species into the soil to accelerate the decomposition of fungal-harboring crop residues.
Prune low hanging leaves to reduce contact with soil splash.
chemical_control: Spray strobilurin (e.g., pyraclostrobin) or triazole (e.g., prothioconazole) fungicides.
Apply chemical treatments strictly at or before the VT (tasseling) stage if conditions are warm and wet.
prevention_tips: Engage in a 2-year crop rotation schedule away from corn (e.g., rotate with soybeans, alfalfa, or clover).
Perform deep tillage or conservation plowing to bury infested stalks and residue, preventing spore survival.
Use hybrids screened for high tolerance/resistance to GLS (Gray Leaf Spot).

symptoms: Elongated, rusty, golden-brown to cinnamon-brown pustules on both upper and lower surfaces of leaves.
Pustules break open, releasing masses of powdery, brown, wind-borne spores (urediniospores).
Infected leaves show chlorosis (yellowing), necrosis (death), and dry out rapidly.
Pustules turn blackish or dark brown towards the end of the growing season as winter spores (teliospores) form.
organic_control: Apply foliar bio-fungicide sprays containing Bacillus velezensis or extract of Reynoutria sachalinensis.
Dust plants with micro-refined dusting sulfur to establish an inhospitable barrier for spores.
Use drip irrigation to prevent long periods of leaf wetness.
chemical_control: Apply pyraclostrobin, azoxystrobin, trifloxystrobin, or propiconazole-based fungicides.
Treat immediately if infection occurs in the whorl stage or if high disease pressure is forecasted.
prevention_tips: Sow corn varieties with high resistance conferred by the Rp gene family.
Destroy alternative wild weed hosts, particularly Oxalis species (wood sorrel) where sexual reproduction of the fungus occurs.
Sow early in the planting window to establish robust leaves before warm, humid weather triggers rust cycles.
